subject: Physics
q: The total length of potentiometer wire AB is 50 cm in the arrangement as shown in figure. If $P$ is the point where the galvanometer shows zero reading then the length $A P$ is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
question: The total length of potentiometer wire AB is 50 cm in the arrangement as shown in figure. If $P$ is the point where the galvanometer shows zero reading then the length $A P$ is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
solution:
In this potentiometer, for zero galvanometer reading, the potential at P must equal the potential at the junction between the 6Ω and 4Ω resistors.
The voltage distribution across the resistors is $V_{junction} = E \times \frac{6}{6+4} = 0.6E$ from point A.
Since the potentiometer wire is uniform with length 50 cm, the potential distribution is also uniform.
Therefore, $\frac{AP}{AB} = \frac{6}{10}$, giving $AP = 50 \times 0.6 = 30$ cm.

subject: Physics
q: Three voltmeters, all having different internal resistances are joined as shown in figure. When some potential difference is applied across A and B, their readings are V1,V2 and V3. Choose the correct option.
question: Three voltmeters, all having different internal resistances are joined as shown in figure. When some potential difference is applied across A and B, their readings are V1,V2 and V3. Choose the correct option.
solution:
Let's consider the following diagram:

According to the Kirchhoff's voltage law, the algebraic sum of the voltages in a closed loop is zero.

From the above figure, it can be written that

V1+V2-V3=0⇒V1+V2=V3

subject: Physics
q: When an external resistance of $5\ \Omega$ is connected across terminals of a cell, a current of $0.25\text{ A}$ flows through it. When the $5\ \Omega$ resistor is replaced by a $2\ \Omega$ resistor, a current of $0.5\text{ A}$ flows through it. The internal resistance of the cell is _______ $\Omega$.
question: When an external resistance of $5\ \Omega$ is connected across terminals of a cell, a current of $0.25\text{ A}$ flows through it. When the $5\ \Omega$ resistor is replaced by a $2\ \Omega$ resistor, a current of $0.5\text{ A}$ flows through it. The internal resistance of the cell is _______ $\Omega$.
solution:
Let the EMF of the cell be $E$ and its internal resistance be $r$.

The current $I$ in the circuit is given by $I = \dfrac{E}{R + r}$.

For the first case, $R_1 = 5\ \Omega$ and $I_1 = 0.25\text{ A}$:

$0.25 = \dfrac{E}{5 + r} \Rightarrow E = 0.25(5 + r)$

For the second case, $R_2 = 2\ \Omega$ and $I_2 = 0.5\text{ A}$:

$0.5 = \dfrac{E}{2 + r} \Rightarrow E = 0.5(2 + r)$

Equating the expressions for $E$:

$0.25(5 + r) = 0.5(2 + r)$

$5 + r = 2(2 + r)$

$5 + r = 4 + 2r$

$r = 1\ \Omega$

Answer: $1$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement-I : The equivalent emf of two nonideal batteries connected in parallel is smaller than either of the two emfs.
Statement-II : The equivalent internal resistance of two nonideal batteries connected in parallel is smaller than the internal resistance of either of the two batteries.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement-I : The equivalent emf of two nonideal batteries connected in parallel is smaller than either of the two emfs.
Statement-II : The equivalent internal resistance of two nonideal batteries connected in parallel is smaller than the internal resistance of either of the two batteries.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are false
Statement-I is false but Statement-II is true
Both Statement-I and Statement-II are true
Statement-I is true but Statement-II is false
solution:
In parallel connections
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{r_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{1}{r_1}+\frac{1}{r_2} \\
& \frac{E_{\mathrm{eq}}}{r_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{E_1}{r_1}+\frac{E_2}{r_2}
\end{aligned}$
If $E_1=E_2$ and $r_1=r_2, E_{\text {eq }}=E_1=E_2$
$\therefore$ Statement 1 is false.
$r_{\text {eq }}$ is less then both $r_1$ and $r_2$
$\therefore$ Statement II is true

subject: Physics
q: Two cells of emfs $1$ V and $2$ V and internal resistance $2\,\Omega$ and $1\,\Omega$, respectively connected in parallel, gave a current of $1$ A through an external resistance. If the polarity of one cell is reversed, then value of current through the external resistance will be $\dfrac{\alpha}{5}$ A. The value of $\alpha$ is __________.
question: Two cells of emfs $1$ V and $2$ V and internal resistance $2\,\Omega$ and $1\,\Omega$, respectively connected in parallel, gave a current of $1$ A through an external resistance. If the polarity of one cell is reversed, then value of current through the external resistance will be $\dfrac{\alpha}{5}$ A. The value of $\alpha$ is __________.
solution:
For two cells connected in parallel with the same polarity, the equivalent emf $E_{eq}$ and equivalent internal resistance $r_{eq}$ are given by:

$E_{eq} = \dfrac{E_1 r_2 + E_2 r_1}{r_1 + r_2}$

$r_{eq} = \dfrac{r_1 r_2}{r_1 + r_2}$

Substituting the given values ($E_1 = 1$ V, $r_1 = 2\,\Omega$, $E_2 = 2$ V, $r_2 = 1\,\Omega$):

$E_{eq} = \dfrac{1 \times 1 + 2 \times 2}{2 + 1} = \dfrac{5}{3}$ V

$r_{eq} = \dfrac{2 \times 1}{2 + 1} = \dfrac{2}{3}\,\Omega$

The current $I$ through the external resistance $R$ is $1$ A:

$I = \dfrac{E_{eq}}{R + r_{eq}}$

$1 = \dfrac{\dfrac{5}{3}}{R + \dfrac{2}{3}}$

$R + \dfrac{2}{3} = \dfrac{5}{3} \Rightarrow R = 1\,\Omega$

When the polarity of one cell is reversed, the new equivalent emf $E'_{eq}$ becomes:

$E'_{eq} = \dfrac{|E_1 r_2 - E_2 r_1|}{r_1 + r_2} = \dfrac{|1 \times 1 - 2 \times 2|}{3} = \dfrac{3}{3} = 1$ V

The equivalent internal resistance remains the same, $r_{eq} = \dfrac{2}{3}\,\Omega$.

The new current $I'$ through the external resistance $R$ is:

$I' = \dfrac{E'_{eq}}{R + r_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{2}{3}} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{5}{3}} = \dfrac{3}{5}$ A

Given that the new current is $\dfrac{\alpha}{5}$ A, we have:

$\dfrac{\alpha}{5} = \dfrac{3}{5} \Rightarrow \alpha = 3$

Answer: $3$

subject: Physics
q: In the given circuit of potentiometer, the potential difference E across AB(10 m length) is larger than E1 and E2 as well. For key K1 (closed), the jockey is adjusted to touch the wire at point J1 so that there is no deflection in the galvanometer. Now the first battery E1 is replaced by second battery E2 for working by making K1 open and K2 closed. The galvanometer gives then null deflection at J2. The value of E1E2 is a2, where a=_______ .
question: In the given circuit of potentiometer, the potential difference E across AB(10 m length) is larger than E1 and E2 as well. For key K1 (closed), the jockey is adjusted to touch the wire at point J1 so that there is no deflection in the galvanometer. Now the first battery E1 is replaced by second battery E2 for working by making K1 open and K2 closed. The galvanometer gives then null deflection at J2. The value of E1E2 is a2, where a=_______ .
solution:
Length of AB=10 m

For battery E1, balancing length is l1

l1=380 cm [from end A]

For battery E2, balancing length is l2

l2=760 cm [from end A]

Now, we know that E1E2=l1l2

⇒  E1E2=380760=12=ab

a=1

subject: Physics
q:
A current of 2 A flows through a wire of cross-sectional area 25.0 mm2. The number of free electrons in a cubic meter are 2.0×1028. The drift velocity of the electrons is _____ ×10-6 ms-1

(given, charge on electron =1.6×10-19C).
question:
A current of 2 A flows through a wire of cross-sectional area 25.0 mm2. The number of free electrons in a cubic meter are 2.0×1028. The drift velocity of the electrons is _____ ×10-6 ms-1

(given, charge on electron =1.6×10-19C).
solution:
The drift velocity is given by 

vd=IneA

⇒I=nAevd   ...(i)

The given data is 

I=2 AA=25×10-6 m2n=2×1028e=1.6×10-19 C

Substituting the given values in equation (i)

2=2×1028×25×10−6×1.6×10−19×vd⇒vd=22×1028×25×10−6×1.6×10−19

⇒vd=125×1.6×1000 m s-1=25×10−6 m s-1

q: In an ammeter, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the main current passes through the galvanometer. If resistance of the galvanometer is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the resistance of ammeter will be:
question: In an ammeter, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the main current passes through the galvanometer. If resistance of the galvanometer is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the resistance of ammeter will be:
solution:
As potential drop across both branches would be the same, we get ISS=IgG

95100IS=5I100G

⇒S=G19

Now, resistance of ammeter will be

RA=SGS+G=G21920G19

⇒RA=G20

q: An electric toaster has resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Ω</mi></math> at room temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. The toaster is connected to a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> supply. If the current flowing through it reaches <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, the temperature attained by toaster is around: (if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><none></none><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mo>∘</mo></mmultiscripts></math> )
question: An electric toaster has resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Ω</mi></math> at room temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. The toaster is connected to a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> supply. If the current flowing through it reaches <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, the temperature attained by toaster is around: (if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><none></none><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mo>∘</mo></mmultiscripts></math> )
solution:
Resistance at 27 °C, RT=27=60 Ω.

Final resistance, RT=2202.75=80 Ω.

Now, we can write R=R0(1+αΔT)

⇒80=601+2×10-4( T-27)

⇒T≈1694°C

q:
A current of 5 A is passing through a non-linear magnesium wire of cross-section 0.04 m2. At every point the direction of current density is at an angle of 60° with the unit vector of area of cross-section.
The magnitude of electric field at every point of the conductor is: (resistivity of magnesium ρ=44×10-8 Ωm)
question:
A current of 5 A is passing through a non-linear magnesium wire of cross-section 0.04 m2. At every point the direction of current density is at an angle of 60° with the unit vector of area of cross-section.
The magnitude of electric field at every point of the conductor is: (resistivity of magnesium ρ=44×10-8 Ωm)
solution:
I=J→·A→=JAcos(θ)
5=J4100×cos(60)
J=5×50=250 A m-2
Now, E→=ρ×J→
=44×10-8×250=11×10-5 V m-1

q: The resistance of a conductor at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math> What will be the temperature coefficient of resistance of the conductor?
question: The resistance of a conductor at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math> What will be the temperature coefficient of resistance of the conductor?
solution:
16=R01+α15-To

20=R01+α100-T0

Assuming To=0°C, as a general convention.

⇒1620=1+α×151+α×100

⇒α=0.003C∘-1

subject: Physics
q: In a meter bridge, as shown in the figure, it is given that resistance $Y=12.5 \Omega$ and that the balance is obtained at a distance $39.5 \mathrm{~cm}$ from end $A$ (by jockey J). After interchanging the resistances $X$ and $Y$, a new balance point is found at a distance $l_2$ from end $A$. What are the values of $X$ and $l_2$ ?
question: In a meter bridge, as shown in the figure, it is given that resistance $Y=12.5 \Omega$ and that the balance is obtained at a distance $39.5 \mathrm{~cm}$ from end $A$ (by jockey J). After interchanging the resistances $X$ and $Y$, a new balance point is found at a distance $l_2$ from end $A$. What are the values of $X$ and $l_2$ ?
solution:
For a balanced meter bridge,
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{\mathrm{X}}{39.5}=\frac{\mathrm{Y}}{(100-39.5)} \\
&\Rightarrow \mathrm{Y}=39.5=\mathrm{X} \times(100-39.5) \\
&or \ldots \mathrm{X}=\frac{12.5 \times 39.5}{60.5}=8.16 \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$
When $\mathrm{X}$ and $\mathrm{Y}$ are interchanged $l_1$ and (100 $-l_1$ ) will also interchange so, $l_2=60.5 \mathrm{~cm}$

subject: Physics
q: In a meter bridge experiment resistances are connected as shown in the figure. Initially resistance P=4 Ω and the neutral point N is at 60 cm from A . Now an unknown resistance R is connected in series to P and the new position of the neutral point is at 80 cm from A . The value of unknown resistance R is -
question: In a meter bridge experiment resistances are connected as shown in the figure. Initially resistance P=4 Ω and the neutral point N is at 60 cm from A . Now an unknown resistance R is connected in series to P and the new position of the neutral point is at 80 cm from A . The value of unknown resistance R is -
solution:
Given initially a resistance P = 4 Ω is connected in the left gap and let Q is connected in the right gap of meter bridge and the null point is obtained at 60 cm,

From the balanced condition of the Wheatstone bridge 

PQ=RS  ...1

Let ρ is the resistance per unit length of meter bridge wire than

For wire AN, P=ρl=40 ρ   ∵ l = 40 cm 

And for NB wire 

Q = ρ100-lQ= ρ100-60=40 ρ  

On substituting the values from equation 1

460=Q40

⇒Q=166=83 Ω

Now an unknown resistance R is connected in series with the 4 Ω resistance and the null point is obtained at l=80 cm 

Now, P = ρ l =80 l

and Q = ρ100-lQ= ρ100-80= 20 ρ

Then from equation 1

 4+R80=Q20⇒ 4+R80=8320  ∵ Q=83⇒4+R80=860  ⇒ R = 203 Ω

q: A current of 15 mA flows in the circuit as shown in figure. The value of potential difference between the points A and B will be
question: A current of 15 mA flows in the circuit as shown in figure. The value of potential difference between the points A and B will be
solution:
Using current division rule, 

Current, i1=510+5×15 mA=5 mA

Now, using Kirchhoff's voltage law- in lower loop,

VA-5i-10i1-10i=VB

Potential difference between A and B is VA-VB=75+50+150=275 V

solution:
10R10+R×12=15×4
On solving
R=10 Ω

subject: Physics
q: Current passing through a wire as function of time is given as $\mathrm{I}(\mathrm{t})=0.02 \mathrm{t}+0.01 \mathrm{~A}$. The charge that will flow through the wire from $t=1 s$ to $t=2 s$ is :
question: Current passing through a wire as function of time is given as $\mathrm{I}(\mathrm{t})=0.02 \mathrm{t}+0.01 \mathrm{~A}$. The charge that will flow through the wire from $t=1 s$ to $t=2 s$ is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{q}=\int \mathrm{idt} \\ & \int_0^2(0.02 \mathrm{t}+0.01) \mathrm{dt}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{q}=\left[0.02 \frac{\mathrm{t}^2}{2}+0.01 \mathrm{t}\right]_1^2 \\ & =0.01(3)+0.01(1) \\ & =0.04 \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$

q: A potentiometer PQ is set up to compare two resistances, as shown in the figure. The ammeter A in the circuit reads 1.0 A when the two-way key K3 is open. The balance point is at a length l1 cm from P when the two-way key K3 is plugged in between 2 and 1, while the balance point is at a length l2 cm from P when the key K3 is plugged in between 3 and 1 . The ratio of two resistances R1R2, is found to be
question: A potentiometer PQ is set up to compare two resistances, as shown in the figure. The ammeter A in the circuit reads 1.0 A when the two-way key K3 is open. The balance point is at a length l1 cm from P when the two-way key K3 is plugged in between 2 and 1, while the balance point is at a length l2 cm from P when the key K3 is plugged in between 3 and 1 . The ratio of two resistances R1R2, is found to be
solution:
When the key is between 2 and 1

Then, emf V1= IR1=xl1. 

When the key is between 3 and 1

Emf, V2=IR1+R2=xl2. 

The ratio of both emf is R1R1+R2=l1l2

On simplifying the above relation, we get the ratio of resitances, R1R2=l1l2-l1.

question: If in the circuit, power dissipation is $150 \mathrm{~W}$, then $\mathrm{R}$ is

subject: Physics
q:
The equivalent resistance of series combination of two resistors is s. When they are connected in parallel, the equivalent resistance is p. If s=np, then the minimum value for n is ______.

(Round off to the Nearest Integer)
question:
The equivalent resistance of series combination of two resistors is s. When they are connected in parallel, the equivalent resistance is p. If s=np, then the minimum value for n is ______.

(Round off to the Nearest Integer)
solution:
R1+R2=s …1

R1R2R1+R2=p …2

R1R2=sp

R1R2=np2

R1+R2=nR1R2R1+R2

R1+R22R1R2=n

for minimum value of n

R1=R2=R

∴n=(2R)2R2=4

q: The equivalent resistance between A and B of the network shown in figure:
question: The equivalent resistance between A and B of the network shown in figure:
solution:
The given circuit can be redrawn as shown below:

Since the circuit is in balanced wheatstone configuration, R2R=3R6R.

The 9 Ω resistor can be omitted. Now, the circuit has two branches having two resistors connected in series. 

Therefore, the resistance of upper branch is R+3R=4R and lower branch is 2R+6R=8R.

The equivalent resistance will therefore be:

Req=4R8R4R+8R=32R212R=83R

What is the value of the resistance which can convert this galvanometer into a voltmeter given full scale deflection for a potential difference of <math><mn>10</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>?</mo></math>
question: A galvanometer having a coil resistance <math><mn>100</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> gives a full scale deflection when a current of <math><mn>1</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is passed through it. What is the value of the resistance which can convert this galvanometer into a voltmeter given full scale deflection for a potential difference of <math><mn>10</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>?</mo></math>
solution:
Vg=igRg=0.1V
V=10V
R=RgVVg-1
=100×99=9.9KΩ

q: A galvanometer having a resistance of $20 \Omega$ and 30 division on both sides has figure of merit 0.005 ampere/ division. The resistance that should be connected in series such that it can be used as a voltmeter upto 15 volt, is:
question: A galvanometer having a resistance of $20 \Omega$ and 30 division on both sides has figure of merit 0.005 ampere/ division. The resistance that should be connected in series such that it can be used as a voltmeter upto 15 volt, is:
solution:
Deflection current

$=\operatorname{Ig}_{\max }=n x k=0.005 \times 30$

Where, $\mathrm{n}=$ Number of divisions $=30$ and $\mathrm{k}=0.005 \mathrm{amp} /$

division

$\begin{array}{l}

=15 \times 10^{-2}=0.15 \\

\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}}[20+\mathrm{R}] \\

15=0.15[20+\mathrm{R}] \\

100=20+\mathrm{R} \\

\mathrm{R}=80 \Omega

\end{array}$

q: The charge flowing in a conductor changes with time as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>β</mi><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>γ</mi><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>β</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi></math> are constants. Minimum value of current is:
question: The charge flowing in a conductor changes with time as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>β</mi><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>γ</mi><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>β</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi></math> are constants. Minimum value of current is:
solution:
Given here, Q=αt-βt2+γt3

Current flowing through conductor is given by 

i=dQdt=α-2βt+3γt2    ...1

For current to be minimum, didt=0

So, didt=6γt-2β=0

⇒t=β3γ

Substituting in equation (1),

i=α-2βt+3γt2=α-2ββ3γ+3γβ3γ2=α-2β23γ+3β29γ=α-β23γ

q: A current through a wire depends on time as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>i</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>α</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>α</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Find the charge crossed through a section of the wire in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A current through a wire depends on time as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>i</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>α</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>α</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Find the charge crossed through a section of the wire in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
i=20t+8t2

i=dqdt⇒∫dq=∫idt

⇒q=∫01520t+8t2dt

q=20t22+8t33015

q=10×152+81533

q=2250+9000

q=11250 C

subject: Physics
q: As shown in the figure the voltmeter reads 2 V across 5 Ω resistor. The resistance of the voltmeter is _____ Ω
question: As shown in the figure the voltmeter reads 2 V across 5 Ω resistor. The resistance of the voltmeter is _____ Ω
solution:
Let the resistance of the voltmeter be R Ω. The voltmeter is in parallel with the 5 Ω. So the total resistance in the circuit is 

5R5+R+2 Ω

Applying Ohm's law in the whole circuit, V=IR,

I=3 2+5R5+R A

It is given that the potential difference across 5 Ω is 2 V.

Thus, by Ohm's law,

32+5R5+R×5RR+5=2⇒15+3R10+7R×5R5+R=2⇒15R=20+14R⇒R=20 Ω

subject: Physics
q: A galvanometer having coil resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> shows a full scale deflection for a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math>. For it to measure a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, the value of the shunt should be:
question: A galvanometer having coil resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> shows a full scale deflection for a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math>. For it to measure a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, the value of the shunt should be:
solution:
Given G=10 Ω, Ig=3 mA and I=8 A.

Let's consider the following figure:

From the figure, we have

IgG=I-IgS⇒S=IgGI-Ig⇒S=3×10-3108-0.003=3.75×10-3 Ω

subject: Physics
q: In the experiment to determine the galvanometer resistance by half-deflection method, the plot of $1 / \theta$ vs the resistance $(\mathrm{R})$ of the resistance box is shown in the figure. The figure of merit of the galvanometer is _____ $\times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~A} /$ division. [The source has emf 2V]
question: In the experiment to determine the galvanometer resistance by half-deflection method, the plot of $1 / \theta$ vs the resistance $(\mathrm{R})$ of the resistance box is shown in the figure. The figure of merit of the galvanometer is _____ $\times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~A} /$ division. [The source has emf 2V]
solution: $\begin{aligned} & i=K \theta \\ & \frac{2}{G+R}=K \theta \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\theta}=\frac{(G+R) K}{2}=R\left(\frac{K}{2}\right)+\frac{K G}{2} \\ & \text { Slope }=\frac{K}{2}=\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow K=0.5=5 \times 10^{-1} A\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Three resistors of $4 \Omega, 6 \Omega$ and $12 \Omega$ are connected in parallel and the combination is connected in series with a $1.5 \mathrm{~V}$ battery of $1 \Omega$ internal resistance. The rate of Joule heating in the $4 \Omega$ resistor is
question: Three resistors of $4 \Omega, 6 \Omega$ and $12 \Omega$ are connected in parallel and the combination is connected in series with a $1.5 \mathrm{~V}$ battery of $1 \Omega$ internal resistance. The rate of Joule heating in the $4 \Omega$ resistor is
solution:
Resistors $4 \Omega, 6 \Omega$ and $12 \Omega$ are connected in parallel, its equivalent resistance $(R)$ is given by

$$
\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{12} \Rightarrow R=\frac{12}{6}=2 \Omega
$$
Again $R$ is connected to $1.5 \mathrm{~V}$ battery whose internal resistance $r=1 \Omega$.
Equivalent resistance now,
$$
R^{\prime}=2 \Omega+1 \Omega=3 \Omega
$$
Current, $I_{\text {total }}=\frac{V}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{1.5}{3}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~A}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& I_{\text {total }}=\frac{1}{2}=3 x+2 x+x=6 x \\
& \Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{12}
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore$ Current through $4 \Omega$ resistor $=3 x$
$$
=3 \times \frac{1}{12}=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{~A}
$$
Therefore, rate of Joule heating in the $4 \Omega$ resistor
$$
=I^2 R=\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^2 \times 4=\frac{1}{4}=0.25 \mathrm{~W}
$$

q: The resistance of a galvanometer is <math><mn>50</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and the maximum current which can be passed through it is <math><mn>0.002</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>.</mo></math> What resistance must be connected to it in order to convert it into an ammeter of range <math><mn>0</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>0.5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>?</mo></math>
question: The resistance of a galvanometer is <math><mn>50</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and the maximum current which can be passed through it is <math><mn>0.002</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>.</mo></math> What resistance must be connected to it in order to convert it into an ammeter of range <math><mn>0</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>0.5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>?</mo></math>
solution:
RGig=Rsis
50×0.002=Rs0.498
Rs=0.2 Ohm

subject: Physics
q: To know the resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math> of a galvanometer by half deflection method, a battery of emf <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></mrow></msub></math> and resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is used to deflect the galvanometer by angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></math> . If a shunt of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> is needed to get half deflection the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> are related by the equation:
question: To know the resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math> of a galvanometer by half deflection method, a battery of emf <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></mrow></msub></math> and resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is used to deflect the galvanometer by angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></math> . If a shunt of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> is needed to get half deflection the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> are related by the equation:
solution:
Let resistance S is connected with galvanometer of resistance G, than

IG=VR+G


Now, as shown below figure a shunt S is connected in parallel with the galvanometer for half deflection in it, that is IG' = IG2, than

 I=VR+GSG+S


As, shunt and galvanometer are in parallel  then VG=VS
IG'G=I-IG'S

⇒IG'G+S=IS
⇒IG2=ISG+S     ∵IG'=IG2

On, substituting the values of IG and I in above equation

V2R+G=VR+GSG+S×SG+S
⇒12R+G=SRG+S+GS
⇒RG+S+GS=2SR+G
⇒RG+RS+GS=2SR+G
⇒RG=2SR+G-SR+G
⇒RG=SR+G
